refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from the combined design of the NGHE solution, not a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution provides a mechanism (e.g., telemetry, onboard instrumentation, data logging) to capture energy_used and ton_moved over the evaluation period, and that reported values can be independently validated against ground truth measurements.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
The NGHE solution shall measure total work output (mass moved or equivalent productivity metric) during operation.
The NGHE solution shall calculate and report the energy_per_ton ratio from measured energy and work output.
The NGHE solution shall provide access to energy efficiency data for analysis by authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance/Quality, System Engineer).

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio asynchronously via independent metering and data aggregation layer.
DP2: Embed energy sensors in prime mover; embed load sensors on transport mechanism; fuse signals in real-time controller; compute and log energy_per_ton locally with periodic reporting.

DP3: Establish energy accounting via fuel consumption logs (manual or automated pump records) and work output via independent weighbridge or load cell array; reconcile via post-shift batch calculation.

DP4: Deploy distributed sensor network (energy, load, position) with edge compute nodes; aggregate via message broker; compute energy_per_ton in centralized analytics service with configurable evaluation windows.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Deploy distributed sensor network (energy, load, position) with edge compute nodes; aggregate via message broker; compute energy_per_ton in centralized analytics service with configurable evaluation windows.

potential_sources: Sensor calibration drift across distributed nodes—mitigated by centralized validation logic and periodic recalibration scheduling.
Message broker availability—mitigated by local buffering at edge nodes and eventual consistency model.
Clock synchronization across edge nodes—mitigated by timestamp reconciliation in analytics service.
Tight coupling between sensor hardware and edge compute—mitigated by abstraction layer (sensor adapter interface) allowing sensor swaps without compute logic change.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves loose coupling between energy measurement, work output measurement, and ratio computation by introducing an explicit aggregation and analytics layer. This allows independent evolution of sensor types, edge hardware, and reporting logic. DP1 risks tight coupling if metering devices lack standard interfaces. DP2 couples prime mover and transport mechanism design, limiting reuse. DP3 introduces manual reconciliation risk and poor real-time visibility.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Sensor accuracy and calibration procedures are defined by worksite safety plan. Risk: If not, energy_per_ton metric becomes unreliable. Mitigation: Require explicit sensor spec and QA protocol as precondition.

Assumption: Edge compute nodes have sufficient local storage and processing for buffering during message broker outages. Risk: Data loss if buffer overflows. Mitigation: Define buffer sizing based on expected message rate and acceptable latency.

Assumption: Evaluation period is known and stable (e.g., shift, day, week). Risk: If evaluation window changes frequently, analytics service must support dynamic windowing without redeployment. Mitigation: Parameterize evaluation period in configuration, not code.

Assumption: Work output is measurable at a single point (e.g., weighbridge). Risk: If tonnage is distributed across multiple delivery points, aggregation logic becomes complex. Mitigation: Clarify whether all work output converges to one measurement point or requires distributed load sensing.

Assumption: Energy source is metered (fuel pump, electrical meter). Risk: If energy is estimated indirectly (e.g., engine hours × assumed consumption), accuracy degrades. Mitigation: Require direct energy measurement, not proxy.

Assumption: Acceptance criteria `energy_per_ton ≤ E_target` is evaluated over a contiguous period. Risk: If evaluation includes idle time or non-productive cycles, ratio inflates. Mitigation: Define whether E_target applies to all cycles or only productive work cycles; implement filtering logic accordingly.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable continuous measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output, computed as `energy_per_ton = total_energy_used / total_tonnage_moved`, evaluated over operator-configurable time windows, with sensor data aggregated from distributed measurement points and validated against acceptance criterion `energy_per_ton ≤ E_target`. Measurement shall exclude idle and non-productive cycles per worksite operational policy. DP4 supports this by decoupling sensor acquisition (edge), data transport (message broker), and analytics (centralized service), allowing independent scaling and technology substitution while maintaining a single source of truth for the energy_per_ton metric.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Distributed sensor network deployment, edge compute orchestration, message broker integration, and centralized analytics service for energy efficiency metrics computation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingestion of energy, load, and position sensor data from distributed network nodes

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Configuration commands for evaluation windows and sensor network parameters to edge compute nodes

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Message broker connectivity and inter-node communication for distributed sensor aggregation

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the natural home for distributed energy monitoring, analytics, and efficiency computation. Sensor network deployment, edge compute orchestration, message broker integration, and centralized analytics all align with NGHE's core mission. Minor diffusion only through Telemetry ingestion interface and Tasking configuration egress.

Poor fit: Vehicle module is entity-focused and would not naturally own distributed infrastructure, message brokers, or centralized analytics services. Sensor data collection might reside on vehicle, but aggregation, edge compute orchestration, and analytics would diffuse across system boundaries.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is operator-interaction focused. Distributed sensor networks, edge compute, and analytics infrastructure are orthogonal to remote control concerns. High diffusion across multiple system layers.

Poor fit: Safety Services would consume energy efficiency metrics but should not own the underlying sensor network, message broker, or analytics infrastructure. Safety-specific alerting might use energy data, but the DP's core infrastructure belongs elsewhere.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate architectural boundary for DP4 because it is explicitly responsible for energy efficiency optimization and monitoring. The distributed sensor network, edge compute nodes, message broker aggregation, and centralized analytics service are all infrastructure components that directly support NGHE's functional mission. Containment is strong (0.92) because all core elements—deployment, orchestration, aggregation, and computation—remain within the module boundary. Diffusion is minimal (0.08) and limited to well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry input, Tasking output, Network Link connectivity).
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module owns onboard sensors, sensor data collection logic may be distributed across Vehicle and NGHE Solution, creating coupling at the data source. Mitigation: Define clear sensor data contract at Vehicle-NGHE boundary via Telemetry interface.

Risk: Safety Services may need real-time access to energy metrics for safety-critical decisions. If Safety Services requires low-latency energy data, message broker latency or analytics window delays could create coupling. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert interface for critical energy thresholds; decouple safety-critical alerts from analytics window computation.

Risk: Edge compute node configuration and lifecycle management may require coordination with Vehicle or Teleoperation modules if nodes are vehicle-resident. Mitigation: Encapsulate edge node management within NGHE Solution; expose only high-level configuration via Tasking interface.

Risk: Message broker infrastructure may be shared across multiple NGHE Solution subsystems or other system components, creating hidden coupling. Mitigation: Establish Network Link as the sole interface for broker access; document broker contract explicitly.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete technical solution: distributed sensors measure energy and load; edge compute + message broker aggregate data; centralized analytics computes energy_per_ton over configurable windows. The DP is solution-complete and logically sound for the stated functional requirement.

diffusion_risks: Sensor data collection logic may split between Vehicle module (onboard sensor drivers) and NGHE Solution (data contract/ingestion). Mitigation: Enforce Telemetry interface as single contract point; Vehicle owns sensor hardware only, NGHE owns data semantics.

Safety Services may require real-time energy thresholds decoupled from analytics evaluation windows. If Safety Services directly queries analytics service, coupling emerges. Mitigation: Create explicit SafetyAlert interface for critical energy events; keep Safety Services independent of analytics window computation.

Edge compute node lifecycle (provisioning, updates, decommissioning) may require coordination with Vehicle or Teleoperation if nodes are vehicle-resident. Mitigation: Encapsulate all edge node orchestration within NGHE Solution; expose only Tasking interface for configuration.

Message broker infrastructure may be shared across NGHE subsystems or other system services, creating implicit coupling. Mitigation: Designate Network Link as sole broker access interface; document broker contract (topics, schemas, QoS) explicitly.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves strong containment (0.92) with minimal diffusion (0.08). All core DP elements—distributed sensor deployment, edge compute orchestration, message broker integration, and centralized analytics—naturally reside within NGHE's mission boundary. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, Network Link inout) are well-defined and minimal. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are appropriately excluded from ownership. Recommended: Document the four diffusion risks above as architectural constraints in the NGHE Solution design specification.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over agreed evaluation period' is directly computable from DP4 outputs (energy_used from sensors, ton_moved from load sensors, evaluation window from configuration). Centralized analytics service can emit energy_per_ton metric with timestamp and window metadata. Verification requires: (1) sensor calibration/accuracy specs, (2) evaluation window definition in Tasking interface, (3) analytics service test harness for metric computation. All are within NGHE Solution scope.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR allocation to DP4 is unambiguous: measurement (distributed sensors) → aggregation (message broker) → reporting (centralized analytics with configurable windows). IM allocation to NGHE Solution is justified by containment analysis (0.92 score). Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints (not owned by NGHE Solution) but must be respected in evaluation window definition and alert thresholds. Recommend: Add explicit traceability matrix linking FR → DP4 → NGHE Solution modules (sensor network, edge compute, message broker, analytics service) in design documentation.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Telemetry interface contract: define sensor data schema (energy in kWh, load in tons, position in coordinates), sampling rate, and data quality expectations. Clarify Vehicle module owns sensor hardware; NGHE Solution owns data ingestion and semantics.

Formalize Tasking interface contract: specify evaluation window configuration (duration, start/end time, aggregation method), sensor network parameter updates (sampling intervals, thresholds), and response acknowledgment protocol.
Create explicit SafetyAlert interface for critical energy thresholds: decouple real-time safety alerts from analytics window computation. Define latency SLA and alert payload schema.

Document Network Link interface: specify message broker contract (topic names, message schemas, QoS levels, retention policies) and inter-node communication protocol. Establish single point of broker access within NGHE Solution.

Add edge compute node lifecycle management specification: define provisioning, configuration, monitoring, and decommissioning workflows. Ensure all orchestration logic resides within NGHE Solution; expose only high-level Tasking commands.

Define energy_per_ton metric computation algorithm in analytics service: specify handling of partial evaluation windows, sensor failures, and outlier data. Include test cases validating metric against acceptance criterion (≤ E_target).
Establish sensor calibration and accuracy requirements: specify acceptable error margins for energy and load measurements to ensure energy_per_ton metric reliability.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. DP4 is solution-complete and logically sound; NGHE Solution achieves strong containment (0.92) with well-defined external interfaces; verification is feasible via centralized analytics metric computation. However, safety criticality is HIGH, and the recommended revisions expose critical interface contracts and edge compute orchestration logic that require explicit sub-level decomposition to ensure safety-critical energy thresholds, real-time alerting, and sensor data integrity are properly architected. Decomposing into sub-FRs for (1) SafetyAlert interface and real-time threshold enforcement, (2) Telemetry data contract and sensor network management, (3) edge compute lifecycle and orchestration, and (4) analytics metric computation with failure handling will reduce architectural risk and clarify ownership boundaries before detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Enforce real-time energy safety thresholds independent of analytics evaluation windows, with latency SLA ≤ [TBD] ms and alert payload schema defined.
FR4.2: Ingest, validate, and semantically normalize distributed sensor telemetry (energy in kWh, load in tons, position in coordinates) with defined sampling rate and data quality expectations.
FR4.3: Provision, configure, monitor, and decommission edge compute nodes with orchestration logic fully encapsulated within NGHE Solution and exposed only via Tasking interface.

FR4.4: Compute energy_per_ton metric over configurable evaluation windows with explicit handling of partial windows, sensor failures, outliers, and validation against acceptance criterion (≤ E_target).

revise.why: DP4 is logically sound but underspecifies critical safety-critical and architectural details. Revise DP4 to explicitly reference sub-DPs for: (1) SafetyAlert decoupling mechanism (separate real-time threshold service vs. analytics window computation), (2) Telemetry interface contract (schema, sampling, QoS), (3) edge compute orchestration (provisioning, lifecycle, configuration exposure via Tasking only), (4) analytics metric algorithm (failure modes, outlier handling, partial window logic). This ensures DP4 remains the parent solution concept while sub-DPs detail the safety-critical and architectural packaging decisions.

assumptions: Safety-critical energy thresholds can be decoupled from analytics evaluation windows without loss of functional coherence.
Edge compute nodes are vehicle-resident or co-located with distributed sensors; orchestration is fully owned by NGHE Solution.
Telemetry data contract (schema, sampling rate, QoS) can be formalized as a single interface point between Vehicle and NGHE Solution.
Evaluation window configuration (duration, start/end, aggregation method) is provided by external Tasking interface and does not require real-time dynamic adjustment during operation.
Sensor calibration and accuracy requirements can be specified independently and validated before deployment.
open_risks: SafetyAlert interface latency SLA not yet defined; if real-time threshold enforcement requires sub-100ms response, edge compute architecture may require local threshold logic, increasing diffusion risk.

Sensor data quality expectations (acceptable error margins, outlier thresholds) not yet quantified; if energy_per_ton metric is sensitive to sensor noise, calibration and filtering logic may need to reside in edge compute or Vehicle module, creating diffusion.
Edge compute node lifecycle management (provisioning, updates, decommissioning) may require coordination with Vehicle or Teleoperation if nodes are vehicle-resident; ownership boundary unclear.

Message broker infrastructure may be shared across NGHE subsystems or other system services; single-point-of-access enforcement (Network Link interface) may be difficult if broker is managed externally.

Partial evaluation window handling (e.g., if evaluation period ends mid-operation) may require policy decisions from Safety Services or Worksite Operations; analytics service may lack authority to decide metric validity.
evidence_needed: Formal Telemetry interface specification: sensor data schema (energy units, load units, position format), sampling rate (Hz or interval), data quality SLA (accuracy ±%, latency, completeness).

Formal Tasking interface specification: evaluation window configuration (duration, start/end time, aggregation method), sensor network parameter updates (sampling intervals, thresholds), response acknowledgment protocol.
SafetyAlert interface specification: critical energy threshold definition, latency SLA (ms), alert payload schema, and decoupling mechanism from analytics window computation.
Edge compute node lifecycle specification: provisioning workflow, configuration management, monitoring/health checks, decommissioning procedure, and orchestration API exposed via Tasking.

Analytics metric computation algorithm: pseudocode or formal specification for energy_per_ton calculation, handling of partial windows, sensor failure modes, outlier detection/filtering, and validation test cases.
Sensor calibration and accuracy requirements: acceptable error margins (±% or absolute units) for energy and load measurements, calibration procedure, and validation protocol.
Message broker contract specification: topic names, message schemas (Avro/Protobuf), QoS levels (at-least-once, exactly-once), retention policies, and inter-node communication protocol.
